prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
Okay, let me think about that. I think I have shared one before, and it's a word that describes a high level of customer service, or the experience of service and being served by an organization in such a personal and engaging manner that seems to transcend the norms that normally govern human interactions, and as such it's untranslatable. I think that's a good description for what a German company doing business with hundreds of millions of customers would have to have at the highest level of service. The word is "Vorsprung durch Technik," and it almost transcends the requirement of a human to serve a technical system at a very high level of complexity and demand that system be at its highest level of functionality and performance. It translates to [English translation] "Achieved through technology" or "Performance achieved through technology and service design." The term refers to an asset that is unique to a certain company that's the result of them combining their best ideas to design something that could provide a level of service that is considered the highest. When a German company delivers to its customers a very high level of service through engineering and design combined with human interaction and services, the result is called "Vorsprung durch Technik."? [End of translation]]

As you saw, the untranslated word refers to a high level of customer service accomplished through technology and human interaction. I talked about this before with my previous constituent. Because it's not an easy word to translate, some of the challenges with this word means that I don't operate at a high enough level of complexity and customer interactions to allow what I'm doing to be considered a "Vorsprung," even if I ensure that I'm delivering extremely high service. The fact that I still haven't fully translated it indicates that maybe some of my operations are slightly below the level that I have aspired to have for the past few years of my career. The challenge for anyone who serves customers at the highest level of complexity and interactivity is delivering that same level of service across the broadest range of customers. It's something that every large company activated by hundreds of millions of different individuals would be striving to provide if they could serve on all of them simultaneously, but not many can deliver that. My translation of the term "Vorsprung durch Technik" and the business practices in the company that the term refers to suggests that this will not result in a specific unique method or system. Instead, what you'd have is many different approaches reminiscent of the original one now exist. And yet when I examine my newest customers, that kind of personalization and range of options, to see that bespoke personalized experience that's different for each of them, that's what "Vorsprung durch Technik" means for those customers, and it's still difficult to translate that into a simple high-level equivalent in English, even as I practiced it on some of those customers today.
